news_summary: Innovative natural solutions are advancing environmental protection, with plants filtering microplastics, effective termite control methods, resilient California bees combating colony collapse, and geothermal leasing poised to revolutionize sustainable home heating and cooling.
news_summary_fr: Des solutions naturelles innovantes font progresser la protection de l'environnement : des plantes capables de filtrer les microplastiques, des méthodes efficaces de lutte contre les termites, des abeilles californiennes résistantes qui combattent l'effondrement des colonies, ainsi que des concessions géothermiques appelées à révolutionner le chauffage et la climatisation durables des habitations.
news_summary_es: Las soluciones naturales innovadoras están impulsando la protección del medio ambiente, gracias a plantas que filtran los microplásticos, métodos eficaces de control de termitas, abejas californianas resistentes que combaten el síndrome de colapso de las colonias y contratos de arrendamiento geotérmico que prometen revolucionar la calefacción y la refrigeración sostenibles de los hogares.

articles.title: This common plant could clean microplastics from your drinking water
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that moringa seeds can help pull microplastics out of water, rivaling standard chemical treatments. The plant-based extract causes plastic particles to clump together, making them easier to filter away. In some conditions, it even outperformed conventional chemicals. This low-cost, natural solution could be a game-changer for cleaner drinking water, especially in smaller communities.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Institute of Science and Technology of São Paulo State University (ICT-UNESP) in São José dos Campos, Brazil, have found that Moringa oleifera, commonly known as moringa or white acacia, could help remove microplastics from water.

The findings were published in ACS Omega, a journal of the American Chemical Society.

Moringa is native to India and grows well in many tropical regions. Its leaves and seeds are widely consumed as food due to their nutritional value. Scientists have also been exploring the seeds for years because of their potential role in water purification.

Moringa Seed Extract Performs Like Chemical Treatment

"We showed that the saline extract from the seeds performs similarly to aluminum sulfate, which is used in treatment plants to coagulate water containing microplastics. In more alkaline waters, it performed even better than the chemical product," says Gabrielle Batista, the first author of the study. She carried out the research during her master's degree in the Post-Graduate Program in Civil and Environmental Engineering (PPGECA) at the Bauru School of Engineering (FEB) at UNESP.

The study was led by Adriano Gonçalves dos Reis, a professor at ICT-UNESP and in the PPGECA at FEB-UNESP. He also heads the project "Direct and In-Line Filtration for the Removal of Microplastics from Drinking Water," which is funded by FAPESP.

"The only drawback found so far regarding aluminum sulfate was the increase in dissolved organic matter, the removal of which could make the process more expensive. However, on a small scale, such as on rural properties and in small communities, the method could be used cost-effectively and efficiently," says Reis.

How Coagulation Helps Remove Microplastics

The research focused on in-line filtration, a method in which water is first treated with a coagulant and then passed through a sand filter. This approach works best for water with low turbidity, meaning it is relatively clear and requires fewer preliminary treatment steps.

Coagulation plays a key role because microplastics and other contaminants carry a negative electrical charge. This causes them to repel each other and prevents them from being captured easily during filtration. Coagulants, including moringa salt extract (which can be made at home) and aluminum sulfate, neutralize these charges. As a result, the particles stick together, forming larger clusters that can be removed more easily.

Earlier work by the same research group showed that moringa seeds are effective throughout a full treatment cycle, including flocculation, sedimentation, and filtration. Luiz Gustavo Rodrigues Godoy, the first author of the study, completed his master's degree at FEB-UNESP with support from FAPESP.

Lab Tests With Microplastic Contaminated Water

To evaluate the method, the team added polyvinyl chloride (PVC) microplastics to tap water.

PVC was chosen because it is considered one of the most harmful plastics for human health, with known mutagenic and carcinogenic properties. It is also commonly found on the surfaces of water bodies and can persist even after conventional treatment.

The researchers exposed the PVC to ultraviolet radiation to simulate natural aging and better reflect the characteristics of real-world microplastics.

The contaminated water then went through coagulation and filtration using a Jar Test system, which mimics water treatment processes on a small scale. The results were compared with samples treated using aluminum sulfate.

To measure effectiveness, the team used scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to count microplastic particles before and after treatment. They also analyzed the size of the particle clusters formed using a high-speed camera and laser measurements. Both treatments showed similar levels of microplastic removal.

Testing Moringa in Real Water Sources

The researchers are now testing moringa seed extract on water collected from the Paraíba do Sul River, which supplies São José dos Campos. So far, the results suggest the method is effective in treating natural water conditions as well.

"There's increasing regulatory scrutiny and health concerns regarding the use of aluminum- and iron-based coagulants, as they aren't biodegradable, leave residual toxicity, and pose a risk of disease. For that reason, the search for sustainable alternatives has intensified," Reis concludes.

The findings highlight moringa as a promising, more sustainable option for reducing microplastics in drinking water, especially in smaller communities where cost and accessibility are key concerns.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that moringa seed extract can effectively remove harmful microplastics from drinking water, rivaling conventional chemical treatments. This natural, low-cost solution has broad environmental and public health implications, especially benefiting smaller communities with limited resources. The article provides detailed context on the research, methodology, and potential impact, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with measurable benefits.
articles.personality_title: Moringa seeds offer natural way to remove microplastics from drinking water
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Microplastics are tiny pieces of plastic that can pollute drinking water and may harm health. Usually, water treatment plants use chemicals like aluminum sulfate to remove these particles, but these chemicals can have drawbacks.

**What happened** – Scientists in Brazil discovered that an extract made from moringa seeds, a common plant also called white acacia, can clean microplastics from water. They tested this by adding harmful PVC microplastics to water and found that moringa seed extract clumped the plastics together just as well as aluminum sulfate, sometimes even better in certain water types. This helps the plastic particles stick together so they can be filtered out more easily.

**Impact** – This finding is important because moringa seed extract is natural, low-cost, and easier to use, especially in small communities or rural areas where expensive chemicals might not be practical. Also, moringa is safe and biodegradable, unlike some chemical treatments that can leave harmful residues.

**What's next step** – The researchers are now testing the moringa seed extract on real river water to confirm it works outside the lab. If successful, this method could become a simple and affordable way to improve drinking water quality in many places.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Moringa seed extract provides a natural, effective, and affordable method to remove harmful microplastics from drinking water, offering a new option for cleaner water worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les graines de moringa, une solution naturelle pour éliminer les microplastiques de l'eau potable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les microplastiques sont de très petites particules de plastique qui polluent l'eau potable et peuvent nuire à la santé. Habituellement, les stations de traitement utilisent des produits chimiques comme le sulfate d'aluminium pour les éliminer, mais ces produits ont des effets secondaires.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques au Brésil ont découvert qu'un extrait de graines de moringa, une plante commune aussi appelée acacia blanc, peut nettoyer les microplastiques dans l'eau. Ils ont testé cela en ajoutant des microplastiques nocifs en PVC dans l'eau et ont constaté que l'extrait de moringa faisait s'agglomérer les plastiques aussi bien que le sulfate d'aluminium, parfois même mieux selon le type d'eau. Cela aide les particules de plastique à se coller pour être filtrées plus facilement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car l'extrait de graines de moringa est naturel, peu coûteux et facile à utiliser, surtout dans les petites communautés ou zones rurales où les produits chimiques chers sont difficiles à utiliser. De plus, le moringa est sûr et biodégradable, contrairement à certains traitements chimiques qui peuvent laisser des résidus nocifs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs testent maintenant l'extrait de moringa sur de l'eau de rivière réelle pour vérifier qu'il fonctionne en conditions naturelles. Si cela réussit, cette méthode pourrait devenir un moyen simple et abordable d'améliorer la qualité de l'eau potable dans de nombreux endroits.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'extrait de graines de moringa offre une méthode naturelle, efficace et économique pour éliminer les microplastiques nocifs de l'eau potable, apportant une nouvelle option pour une eau plus propre dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Las semillas de moringa ofrecen una forma natural de eliminar microplásticos del agua potable
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los microplásticos son pequeñas partículas de plástico que pueden contaminar el agua potable y afectar la salud. Normalmente, las plantas de tratamiento usan químicos como el sulfato de aluminio para eliminarlos, pero estos químicos pueden tener efectos negativos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en Brasil descubrieron que un extracto hecho de semillas de moringa, una planta común también llamada acacia blanca, puede limpiar microplásticos del agua. Probaron esto agregando microplásticos dañinos de PVC al agua y encontraron que el extracto de moringa hacía que los plásticos se agruparan tan bien como el sulfato de aluminio, a veces mejor en ciertos tipos de agua. Esto ayuda a que las partículas plásticas se peguen para filtrarlas más fácil.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque el extracto de semillas de moringa es natural, barato y fácil de usar, especialmente en comunidades pequeñas o rurales donde los químicos caros no son prácticos. Además, la moringa es segura y biodegradable, a diferencia de algunos tratamientos químicos que pueden dejar residuos dañinos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están probando ahora el extracto de moringa en agua de río real para confirmar que funciona fuera del laboratorio. Si tiene éxito, este método podría ser una forma sencilla y económica de mejorar la calidad del agua potable en muchos lugares.

**Resumen en una frase** – El extracto de semillas de moringa ofrece un método natural, efectivo y accesible para eliminar microplásticos dañinos del agua potable, brindando una nueva opción para agua más limpia en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a cluster of moringa seed pods gently releasing fine, glowing particles into a clear stream, where tiny translucent plastic fragments visibly clump together and settle onto smooth river stones beneath soft natural light.

articles.title: 95% success rate: This new trick lures termites straight to their death
articles.summary: Scientists at UC Riverside have found a clever new way to outsmart termites—by turning their own instincts against them. Using a natural pine scent called pinene, which smells like food to termites, researchers can lure the pests straight toward a targeted dose of insecticide hidden in wood. The result is dramatically higher kill rates—jumping from about 70% to over 95%—without the need for widespread toxic fumigation.
articles.content:
Here is a substantially reworded, engagement-optimized version for a general audience, with the original facts preserved and a light update in context.

Scientists at UC Riverside have identified a cheaper, highly effective way to kill western drywood termites by drawing them toward insecticide instead of trying to track down every hiding place. In experiments tied to a study in the Journal of Economic Entomology, the team used pinene, a pleasant-smelling compound released by forest trees, to make the insects move toward treated wood. The result was a major jump in effectiveness, from about 70% mortality with insecticide alone to more than 95% when pinene was added.

"We saw significant differences in the death rates using insecticide alone versus the insecticide plus pinene," said UCR entomologist Dong-Hwan Choe, who led the discovery. "Without pinene, we got about 70% mortality. When we added it in, it was over 95%."

Why western drywood termites are such a problem

Western drywood termites are native to North America and play a real role in nature by helping break down dead wood above ground with help from microorganisms in their guts. "They are recyclers," Choe said. "And they're very common."

That ecological role becomes a serious headache when the same insects move from dead trees into houses. They do not distinguish between natural wood and structural lumber, which is why they are a persistent concern in places such as California and Florida, as well as parts of Canada and Mexico. "It's only a matter of time before termites attack a house, especially in warmer parts of the states," Choe said.

The limits of fumigation

One of the most widely used treatments for drywood termites is fumigation, in which a home is sealed under a tent and filled with gas to kill the insects. California relies on this approach more than any other U.S. state, according to UC Riverside.

But fumigation has drawbacks. The industry has been under pressure to find alternatives because sulfuryl fluoride, the chemical used in fumigation, is toxic to humans and also acts as a greenhouse gas. It is also costly, and it does not stop a home from becoming reinfested later.

"Even though it is very thorough, a home can be infested again soon after fumigation is completed," Choe said. "Some people fumigate every three to five years because it doesn't protect structures from future infestations."

How pinene helps insecticide work better

Another option is localized injection. In this method, pest control workers drill into infested wood, reach the termite "gallery" or hiding area, and inject insecticide directly inside. Because it targets a smaller area, localized treatment can use fewer chemicals and cost less than fumigation. It may also leave treated wood better protected against future infestations.

"This is a more localized treatment, and in theory, it is a better strategy when you want to control drywood termites with fewer chemicals. It's less expensive, and the treated wood may also stay protected from future infestations," Choe said.

The big challenge is finding where the termites actually are inside the wood. These insects can spread through complex tunnel systems, and standard contact insecticides only work if the termites physically touch the treated area. That is where pinene changed the equation. The scent appears to draw termites from a distance, reducing the need to pinpoint every hidden pocket before treatment.

"Even at low concentrations, pinene is good at attracting termites from a distance," Choe said.

"We don't think it's functioning as a pheromone," Choe said. "We think the scent is more associated with their food. Smells nice… dinner time! That's the concept that we had in mind."

The insecticide used in the study

The insecticide used in the 2024 study was fipronil, which is also used against ants. Fipronil can harm aquatic insects and pollinators if it escapes into the environment, but in this case it was injected into wood, which lowers the chance of off-target effects.

Choe's lab focuses on how urban insect pests communicate chemically, with the goal of turning that knowledge into smarter control strategies for pests such as western drywood termites.

"Our study shows that if you understand insect behavior better, it's interesting by itself," Choe said. "Then there are also important implications for more effective pest management, so we can use fewer chemicals without compromising efficiency."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in pest control that improves termite management by increasing kill rates from 70% to over 95% using a natural attractant, pinene, combined with localized insecticide treatment. This method reduces the need for widespread toxic fumigation, which has environmental and health drawbacks, and offers a more cost-effective, targeted, and environmentally safer alternative. The impact is broad as termite infestations affect many homeowners and ecosystems, and the article provides sufficient detail on the discovery, its benefits, and implications.
articles.personality_title: New scent-based method kills 95% of termites without toxic fumigation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Western drywood termites damage wooden structures in many parts of North America. Traditional treatments often rely on fumigation, which uses toxic gas and can be expensive and harmful to the environment.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of California Riverside discovered that adding a natural pine scent called pinene to insecticide draws termites toward treated wood. This method increased termite kill rates from about 70% to over 95% in their experiments.

**Impact** – This new technique targets termites more precisely, reducing the need for widespread fumigation. It uses fewer chemicals and is less costly, while lowering risks to people and the environment. This is important because termites are hard to find inside wood, and fumigation does not prevent future infestations.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to further develop this method and explore its use in real homes. They also want to better understand termite behavior to create smarter, safer pest control options.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that using a natural pine scent with insecticide can kill over 95% of termites, offering a safer and more effective alternative to fumigation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode à base de parfum tue 95 % des termites sans fumigation toxique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les termites secs de l’Ouest endommagent les structures en bois dans plusieurs régions d’Amérique du Nord. Les traitements traditionnels utilisent souvent la fumigation, qui emploie un gaz toxique, coûte cher et nuit à l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Californie à Riverside ont découvert qu’ajouter un parfum naturel de pin, appelé pinène, à un insecticide attire les termites vers le bois traité. Cette méthode a augmenté le taux de mortalité des termites de 70 % à plus de 95 % lors de leurs expériences.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle technique cible les termites plus précisément, réduisant le besoin de fumigation étendue. Elle utilise moins de produits chimiques, coûte moins cher et diminue les risques pour les personnes et l’environnement. C’est important car les termites sont difficiles à repérer dans le bois, et la fumigation ne protège pas contre les futures infestations.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de développer davantage cette méthode et d’explorer son utilisation dans de vraies maisons. Ils veulent aussi mieux comprendre le comportement des termites pour créer des solutions antiparasitaires plus intelligentes et sûres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que l’utilisation d’un parfum naturel de pin avec un insecticide peut tuer plus de 95 % des termites, offrant une alternative plus sûre et efficace à la fumigation.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método con aroma mata al 95 % de las termitas sin fumigación tóxica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las termitas secas del oeste dañan estructuras de madera en varias partes de América del Norte. Los tratamientos tradicionales suelen usar fumigación, que emplea gases tóxicos, es costosa y perjudica el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de California en Riverside descubrieron que agregar un aroma natural de pino llamado pineno al insecticida atrae a las termitas hacia la madera tratada. Este método aumentó la tasa de mortalidad de las termitas de alrededor del 70 % a más del 95 % en sus experimentos.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva técnica apunta a las termitas de forma más precisa, reduciendo la necesidad de fumigación generalizada. Usa menos químicos, cuesta menos y disminuye los riesgos para las personas y el ambiente. Esto es importante porque las termitas son difíciles de encontrar dentro de la madera y la fumigación no previene futuras infestaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean desarrollar más esta técnica y probarla en viviendas reales. También quieren entender mejor el comportamiento de las termitas para crear opciones de control más inteligentes y seguras.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos encontraron que usar un aroma natural de pino con insecticida puede matar más del 95 % de las termitas, ofreciendo una alternativa más segura y efectiva a la fumigación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a cross-section of wooden beams showing intricate termite tunnels with glowing, inviting wisps of pine-scented vapor leading the tiny termite silhouettes toward a softly illuminated area where a subtle, protective shield of insecticide gently envelops them, set against a simple, natural background of muted browns and greens.

articles.title: These California bees are beating a killer that’s wiping out colonies
articles.summary: A unique hybrid honeybee thriving in Southern California may hold a powerful clue to saving struggling bee populations. While U.S. beekeepers are losing massive numbers of colonies—largely due to destructive Varroa mites—a locally adapted mix of feral and diverse bee lineages is showing remarkable resilience. These bees aren’t immune, but they carry far fewer mites and are far less likely to require chemical treatments. Even more surprising, their resistance appears to start early in life, with larvae that are less attractive to the parasites.
articles.content:
Southern California may be home to an unexpected ally in the fight to save honeybees. As commercial hives across the United States struggle to survive attacks from deadly parasites, a distinct hybrid bee found in this region is showing a surprising ability to endure.

Beekeepers across the country reported losing as much as 62% of their managed honeybee colonies in 2025, raising serious concerns about food production. These losses are linked to several pressures, including pesticide exposure, climate stress, shrinking habitats, and паразites. Among the most damaging threats is the Varroa mite.

How Varroa Mites Damage Honeybees

Varroa mites weaken bees by feeding on their fat body tissue, an essential organ that supports immune function, metabolism, and energy storage. If you were comparing it to human biology, it performs roles similar to the liver, pancreas, and immune system. As a result of this damage, bees lose weight, become more vulnerable to disease, and have shorter lifespans.

The mites also spread dangerous viruses such as Deformed Wing Virus and Acute Bee Paralysis Virus by injecting them directly into a bee's bloodstream. To combat infestations, beekeepers often rely on chemical treatments, but these solutions can become less effective over time.

Study Finds Natural Mite Resistance in Hybrid Bees

New research from UC Riverside, published in Scientific Reports, offers a rare piece of good news. The study is the first to demonstrate that a locally adapted group of honeybees can consistently and naturally keep mite populations under control.

"We kept hearing anecdotally that these Californian honeybees were surviving with way fewer treatments. I wanted to test them rigorously and understand the driving force behind what the beekeepers were seeing," said Genesis Chong-Echavez, a UCR graduate student and lead author of the study.

Working with entomologists from UCR's Center for Integrative Bee Research (CIBER), Chong-Echavez tracked 236 honeybee colonies from 2019 through 2022.

Fewer Mites and Less Need for Treatment

The results showed that these bees are not completely resistant, but they perform far better than typical commercial colonies. Colonies led by locally raised hybrid queens carried about 68% fewer mites on average compared to those led by commercial queens. They were also more than five times less likely to reach levels where chemical intervention becomes necessary.

These bees are not part of any commercial breeding program. Instead, they come from a naturally mixed population in Southern California, often originating from feral colonies living in trees. Genetic studies reveal that they combine traits from at least four honeybee lineages, including African, Eastern European, Middle Eastern, and Western European bees.

Larval Stage May Hold the Key

To understand why these bees perform better, researchers conducted lab experiments focusing on developing larvae. Varroa mites must enter brood cells to reproduce, so the team tested whether mites were equally attracted to larvae from different types of colonies.

They were not.

Mites showed less interest in larvae from the hybrid Californian bees, especially at around seven days old, when larvae are usually most vulnerable. This suggests that the bees' defense may begin early in development, before adult behaviors play a role.

"What surprised me most was the differences showed up even at the larval stage," Chong-Echavez said. "This suggests the resistance mechanism may go deeper than some kind of behavior and may be genetically built into the bees themselves."

Implications for Global Honeybee Health

The findings could have significance far beyond Southern California. Honeybees are essential pollinators responsible for crops worth billions of dollars, yet they continue to face mounting environmental pressures. This research points to the possibility that natural biological traits could help strengthen bee populations.

Boris Baer, a UCR entomology professor and co-author of the study, emphasized the importance of collaboration with beekeepers.

"This question did not start in the lab. It started in conversations with beekeepers," Baer said. "They were not just observers; they helped shape the questions behind this research."

What Comes Next

Researchers stress that these hybrid bees are not completely free of mites, and current management practices should not be abandoned. Instead, the goal is to identify the specific traits that allow these bees to keep mite levels lower and explore how those traits could support breeding efforts or reduce reliance on chemicals.

Future studies will focus on uncovering the genetic, behavioral, and chemical signals that may make the larvae less attractive to mites.

"At a time when pollinators are facing global decline, this work offers a hopeful message: solutions may already be emerging in the field, and we just need to understand them," Chong-Echavez said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a naturally resilient hybrid honeybee population in Southern California that shows strong resistance to the destructive Varroa mite, a major threat to global bee populations and food production. This finding has broad implications for improving honeybee health worldwide, which is crucial for ecosystem stability and agriculture. The article provides detailed context on the problem, the research methodology, and the potential impact, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: California hybrid bees show natural resistance to deadly Varroa mites
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Honeybees are important for pollinating many crops, but in recent years, their numbers have dropped sharply in the United States. One of the main reasons is a tiny parasite called the Varroa mite. This mite feeds on bees and spreads harmful viruses, causing many colonies to die. Beekeepers often use chemicals to fight these mites, but these treatments don’t always work well.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of California Riverside studied a special group of hybrid honeybees living in Southern California. These bees come from a mix of different types, including African and European bees, and many come from wild colonies in trees. The researchers followed 236 bee colonies from 2019 to 2022 and found that these hybrid bees had about 68% fewer mites than typical commercial bees. They also needed chemical treatments much less often. The study showed that even young larvae of these bees are less attractive to mites, which might explain their resistance.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that some bees can naturally control mite populations without heavy use of chemicals. The fact that the resistance starts early, at the larval stage, suggests the bees might have built-in genetic defenses. This could help beekeepers breed stronger bees and reduce the damage caused by mites, improving bee health and supporting food production.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to study exactly how these bees resist mites, including their genetics and chemical signals that keep mites away. They want to use this knowledge to help develop new ways to protect bees worldwide. For now, beekeepers should continue current mite control methods but watch for new solutions based on this research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A unique hybrid honeybee population in Southern California naturally limits harmful Varroa mites, offering new clues to protect bees and support agriculture worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les abeilles hybrides de Californie résistent naturellement aux acariens Varroa mortels
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les abeilles sont essentielles pour la pollinisation des cultures, mais leur nombre a fortement diminué aux États-Unis. L’un des principaux responsables est un parasite minuscule appelé acarien Varroa. Cet acarien se nourrit des abeilles et transmet des virus dangereux, provoquant la mort de nombreuses colonies. Les apiculteurs utilisent souvent des produits chimiques pour lutter contre ces acariens, mais ces traitements ne sont pas toujours efficaces.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Californie à Riverside ont étudié un groupe spécial d’abeilles hybrides vivant dans le sud de la Californie. Ces abeilles sont issues d’un mélange de plusieurs types, notamment africain et européen, et beaucoup proviennent de colonies sauvages dans les arbres. Les chercheurs ont suivi 236 colonies entre 2019 et 2022 et ont découvert que ces abeilles hybrides avaient environ 68 % d’acariens en moins que les abeilles commerciales classiques. Elles avaient aussi beaucoup moins besoin de traitements chimiques. L’étude a montré que même les larves jeunes de ces abeilles attirent moins les acariens, ce qui pourrait expliquer leur résistance.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que certaines abeilles peuvent naturellement contrôler la population d’acariens sans recourir massivement aux produits chimiques. Le fait que la résistance commence dès le stade larvaire suggère que les abeilles ont des défenses génétiques intégrées. Cela pourrait aider les apiculteurs à élever des abeilles plus fortes et à réduire les dégâts causés par les acariens, améliorant ainsi la santé des abeilles et la production alimentaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier précisément comment ces abeilles résistent aux acariens, notamment leurs gènes et les signaux chimiques qui repoussent les acariens. Ils veulent utiliser ces connaissances pour développer de nouvelles méthodes de protection des abeilles dans le monde. Pour l’instant, les apiculteurs doivent continuer à utiliser les méthodes actuelles mais rester attentifs aux nouvelles solutions basées sur cette recherche.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une population unique d’abeilles hybrides en Californie limite naturellement les acariens Varroa nocifs, offrant de nouvelles pistes pour protéger les abeilles et soutenir l’agriculture mondiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Abejas híbridas de California muestran resistencia natural a los mortales ácaros Varroa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las abejas son esenciales para polinizar muchos cultivos, pero en los últimos años su número ha disminuido mucho en Estados Unidos. Una de las principales causas es un pequeño parásito llamado ácaro Varroa. Este ácaro se alimenta de las abejas y transmite virus dañinos, causando la muerte de muchas colonias. Los apicultores suelen usar químicos para combatir estos ácaros, pero estos tratamientos no siempre funcionan bien.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de California en Riverside estudiaron un grupo especial de abejas híbridas que viven en el sur de California. Estas abejas provienen de una mezcla de diferentes tipos, incluyendo africanas y europeas, y muchas vienen de colonias salvajes en árboles. Los investigadores siguieron 236 colonias entre 2019 y 2022 y encontraron que estas abejas híbridas tenían alrededor de un 68% menos de ácaros que las abejas comerciales comunes. También necesitaban tratamientos químicos mucho menos. El estudio mostró que incluso las larvas jóvenes de estas abejas atraen menos a los ácaros, lo que podría explicar su resistencia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que algunas abejas pueden controlar naturalmente la población de ácaros sin usar muchos químicos. El hecho de que la resistencia comience temprano, en la etapa larval, sugiere que las abejas podrían tener defensas genéticas incorporadas. Esto podría ayudar a los apicultores a criar abejas más fuertes y reducir los daños causados por los ácaros, mejorando la salud de las abejas y apoyando la producción de alimentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar cómo estas abejas resisten a los ácaros, incluyendo su genética y las señales químicas que mantienen alejados a los ácaros. Quieren usar este conocimiento para ayudar a desarrollar nuevas formas de proteger a las abejas en todo el mundo. Por ahora, los apicultores deben continuar con los métodos actuales de control de ácaros pero estar atentos a nuevas soluciones basadas en esta investigación.

**Frase clave** – Una población única de abejas híbridas en California limita naturalmente los dañinos ácaros Varroa, ofreciendo nuevas pistas para proteger a las abejas y apoyar la agricultura mundial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant honeybee hive nestled in a sunlit Southern California tree, showing healthy hybrid bees with subtle mixed-color patterns working harmoniously while tiny, shadowy Varroa mites hover at a distance, repelled by glowing, protective larval cells inside the hive.

articles.title: Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Dandelion Energy CEO Dan Yates On How Geothermal Leasing Could Transform Home Heating and Cooling
articles.summary:
Read a transcript of this episode. Subscribe to receive transcripts. Return to one of our...
The post Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Dandelion Energy CEO Dan Yates On How Geothermal Leasing Could Transform Home Heating and Cooling appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Return to one of our most compelling interviews of 2025. Amazingly, the same Congressional bill that gutted residential clean energy tax credits also led to a major breakthrough in financing home geothermal systems. Dan Yates, CEO of Dandelion Energy, explains how the Big, Beautiful Bill introduced changes that, for the first time, allow third-party leasing of residential geothermal systems. He shares why this policy change could help ground-source heat pumps grow the way leasing helped rooftop solar. Geothermal heating and cooling is four times more efficient than a furnace and twice as efficient as air-source heat pumps. Yet only about 1% of U.S. homes use it because the upfront costs for new geothermal systems have ranged from $20,000 to $31,000. The new leasing model means new homeowners can get geothermal systems for just $10 to $40 per month on a 20-year lease, which is usually far less than what they save on energy.

Dandelion is working with Lennar, one of the largest homebuilders in the country, to bring geothermal to more than 1,500 homes in Colorado over the next two years. This will be one of the biggest residential geothermal projects in U.S. history. The benefits for the power grid could be even more important than the savings for homeowners. Geothermal systems use only 25% of the peak power that air-source heat pumps need, which is a big advantage as AI data centers increase electricity demand. Yates explains that the Earth works like a huge thermal battery, storing heat in the summer for use in the winter. Geothermal lets utilities reduce peak loads on the grid throughout the year, freeing homeowners from the cost of the most expensive power.

You can learn more about Dandelion Energy at dandelionenergy.com

Subscribe to Sustainability In Your Ear on iTunes

Follow Sustainability In Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired on December 29, 2025.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy breakthrough enabling third-party leasing of residential geothermal systems, which can greatly increase adoption of highly efficient geothermal heating and cooling. This has broad potential environmental benefits by reducing energy consumption and peak grid loads, with a large-scale project underway involving a major homebuilder. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible positive impact on sustainable home energy use.
articles.personality_title: New Leasing Plan Could Make Geothermal Heating Affordable for Many Homes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Geothermal heating and cooling uses the stable temperature underground to warm or cool homes efficiently. It is much better for saving energy than traditional furnaces or regular heat pumps, but very few U.S. homes have it because installing the systems costs a lot of money upfront.

**What happened** – A new law passed by Congress now allows companies to lease geothermal systems to homeowners. This means instead of paying $20,000 to $31,000 at once, people can pay a smaller monthly fee, about $10 to $40, for 20 years. Dan Yates, CEO of Dandelion Energy, explains how this change opens the door for many more homes to get geothermal heating and cooling. Dandelion Energy is teaming up with Lennar, a big homebuilder, to install geothermal systems in over 1,500 homes in Colorado soon.

**Impact** – This leasing idea is important because it lowers the cost barrier that stopped most people from using geothermal systems before. Geothermal is four times more efficient than furnaces and uses less electricity, which helps reduce stress on the power grid. This is especially helpful as new technologies, like AI data centers, increase electricity demand. Using geothermal heating and cooling can save homeowners money and make the electric system more reliable.

**What’s next step** – The project with Lennar will show how well leasing works on a large scale. If it succeeds, more homebuilders and homeowners across the country might start using geothermal systems this way. This could lead to cleaner, cheaper heating and cooling in many more homes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new leasing option makes geothermal heating affordable and could help thousands of homes save energy and money while easing demand on the power grid.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau modèle de location rend le chauffage géothermique abordable pour de nombreuses maisons
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le chauffage et la climatisation géothermiques utilisent la température stable du sol pour chauffer ou refroidir les maisons de manière efficace. C’est bien plus économique en énergie que les chaudières traditionnelles ou les pompes à chaleur classiques, mais très peu de foyers américains en disposent car l’installation coûte cher.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle loi adoptée par le Congrès permet désormais aux entreprises de louer des systèmes géothermiques aux propriétaires. Au lieu de payer entre 20 000 et 31 000 dollars d’un coup, les gens peuvent verser une petite somme mensuelle, entre 10 et 40 dollars, pendant 20 ans. Dan Yates, PDG de Dandelion Energy, explique comment cette mesure ouvre la voie à beaucoup plus de maisons équipées. Dandelion Energy s’associe avec Lennar, un grand constructeur, pour installer ces systèmes dans plus de 1 500 maisons au Colorado.

**Impact** – Ce système de location est important car il supprime la grande barrière financière qui empêchait auparavant l’adoption de la géothermie. Cette technologie est quatre fois plus efficace qu’une chaudière et consomme moins d’électricité, ce qui aide à réduire la pression sur le réseau électrique. Cela devient crucial avec la montée en puissance des centres de données pour l’intelligence artificielle. La géothermie permet aux propriétaires d’économiser de l’argent tout en améliorant la fiabilité du réseau.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet avec Lennar va montrer si la location fonctionne bien à grande échelle. En cas de succès, d’autres constructeurs et propriétaires à travers le pays pourraient adopter cette solution. Cela pourrait conduire à un chauffage et une climatisation plus propres et moins chers pour beaucoup plus de foyers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle option de location rend le chauffage géothermique accessible et pourrait aider des milliers de foyers à économiser de l’énergie et de l’argent tout en soulageant le réseau électrique.
articles.personality_title_es: Un nuevo modelo de arrendamiento hace que la calefacción geotérmica sea accesible para muchas viviendas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La calefacción y refrigeración geotérmica usa la temperatura constante bajo tierra para calentar o enfriar casas de manera eficiente. Es mucho mejor para ahorrar energía que las calderas tradicionales o las bombas de calor comunes, pero pocas casas en EE. UU. la usan porque instalar estos sistemas cuesta mucho dinero al principio.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva ley del Congreso permite que las empresas alquilen sistemas geotérmicos a los dueños de casas. Así, en lugar de pagar entre 20,000 y 31,000 dólares de una vez, las personas pueden pagar una cuota mensual más baja, entre 10 y 40 dólares, durante 20 años. Dan Yates, CEO de Dandelion Energy, explica cómo este cambio puede ayudar a que muchas más casas tengan calefacción y refrigeración geotérmica. Dandelion Energy se está asociando con Lennar, un gran constructor, para instalar sistemas en más de 1,500 casas en Colorado.

**Impacto** – Este modelo de arrendamiento es importante porque reduce la barrera del alto costo inicial que impedía que muchas personas usaran sistemas geotérmicos. La geotermia es cuatro veces más eficiente que las calderas y usa menos electricidad, lo que ayuda a disminuir la carga en la red eléctrica. Esto es útil especialmente porque nuevas tecnologías, como los centros de datos de inteligencia artificial, aumentan la demanda de electricidad. Usar geotermia puede ahorrar dinero a los dueños de casas y hacer que el sistema eléctrico sea más estable.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto con Lennar mostrará cómo funciona el arrendamiento a gran escala. Si tiene éxito, más constructores y propietarios en todo el país podrían usar sistemas geotérmicos de esta manera. Esto podría llevar a un calentamiento y enfriamiento más limpio y barato en muchas más casas.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva opción de arrendamiento hace que la calefacción geotérmica sea asequible y podría ayudar a miles de hogares a ahorrar energía y dinero mientras alivian la demanda en la red eléctrica.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cozy modern home with stylized earth layers beneath it showing a glowing geothermal loop system gently circulating heat, while a large, sturdy seedling shaped like a house sprouts from the ground nearby, symbolizing sustainable growth and affordable leasing powering clean heating and cooling.
